title: The Culture Gets Made in the First Sixty Seconds After Someone Brings You a Problem.
blurb: A leader pinged me on Teams after making a call I wouldn't have made, watching my face before I said a word. That ten-minute conversation is where culture actually gets built or broken, long before any values workshop. Programs communicate intent. Behavior communicates reality. The person who brought you the problem is already deciding whether they'll bring you the next one.

title: Busyness and Effectiveness Work Against Each Other at the Top.
blurb: Daniel ran a 1,200-person division from back-to-back meetings, 7am to 7pm, and by his own admission wasn't doing any real thinking. At the top, the real work is generative: thinking clearly about where the organization is going and why. He was busy and responsive, and still failing at the one thing his people needed most from him. The strongest executives I've seen protect unscheduled thinking time as a core discipline.

title: Leadership Still Comes Down to Signing Your Name to Something You Can't Fully See Yet.
blurb: 250 years ago, a handful of people signed their names to a document that could have gotten them hanged. No guarantees it would hold. They signed anyway. The job still comes down to putting your name on a decision you can't fully see yet, and owning what happens next.

title: As AI Takes On More Execution, Leadership Has Less Room to Hide.
blurb: AI gives time back, but it does not fix the system around the work. Unclear priorities just move faster, weak decision rights create more noise, and managers still struggle to coach through ambiguity. The leaders who win will redesign the work around better human judgment and agent orchestration, not just adopt tools faster.

title: AI Twins Don't Just Inherit Your Expertise. They Inherit Your Blind Spots.
blurb: Digital counterparts trained on a leader's past decisions are no longer theoretical. But an AI Twin can absorb stress behaviors, shortcuts, and biases along with the judgment. Before we replicate judgment at scale, we have to define what good judgment actually looks like. That is a leadership integrity question, not just a technology one.

title: We Excused a Toxic High Performer Because the Numbers Were Good.
blurb: A sales leader delivered results through intimidation, and we looked away because the numbers held. When we finally parted ways, people who had gone quiet started speaking up. Psychological safety isn't built through culture initiatives. It's built through daily leadership behavior, and integrity first is an operational discipline.
